food_subgroup: Stuffed flatbread roll / kathi roll with marinated grilled chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for malai tikka roll; closest_parent 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM 'Bread and similar products' is the closest defensible parent; no specific kathi-roll or tikka-roll code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to malai tikka roll or kathi roll as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One malai tikka roll typically weighs approximately 220 g and consists of a paratha or roti wrapped around creamy tandoor-grilled chicken tikka pieces with onions, green chutney, and lemon juice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Kathi Roll / Tikka Roll — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) entries for tandoori chicken, wheat paratha, and cream-based marinade components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for malai tikka roll as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN 2017 data for: (1) tandoori chicken (~100 g filling, ~165 kcal/100 g, protein 25 g, fat 7 g, carb 2 g); (2) wheat paratha (~80 g, ~326 kcal/100 g, protein 8 g, fat 14 g, carb 44 g); (3) cream/malai marinade contribution (~40 g, ~345 kcal/100 g, fat 35 g). Weighted composite per 100 g of assembled roll (220 g total) calculated. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for micronutrient cross-check on chicken and wheat components.

gi_source.title: Chapati / wheat flatbread GI reference and mixed chicken-wheat meal GI estimation — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for malai tikka roll. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on: (1) wheat chapati/paratha GI of 52–62 per University of Sydney GI Database (wheat flour flatbread entries); (2) protein and fat content of the chicken-cream filling lowers the overall meal GI relative to plain flatbread; (3) composite meal GI estimated conservatively at 62 (medium range). GL per serving = (62 × 49.5 g available carbohydrate per 220 g serving) / 100 = 30.7, rounded to 30.6. Available carbohydrate = carbohydrate_g per 100 g (22.5) × serving size (2.2) − fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~924 mg per roll serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit malai tikka roll consumption to occasional servings, as one roll provides approximately 40% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-salt preparation and avoiding additional chutneys high in sodium can help manage intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate glycemic load (~30.6 per roll) from wheat flatbread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume malai tikka roll in controlled portions, as the wheat paratha base contributes a medium GI of approximately 62 and a glycemic load of ~30.6 per serving; pairing with a salad or low-GI vegetables and choosing a thinner roti over a layered paratha can reduce the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream (malai) marinade (~4.2 g/100 g; ~9.2 g per roll) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful that the cream-based marinade contributes approximately 9 g of saturated fat per roll, approaching 45% of the recommended daily limit of 20 g; choosing a lighter yoghurt-based marinade and limiting portion size to half a roll can reduce saturated fat and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malai tikka roll is a popular North Indian street-food and restaurant dish consisting of tender, creamy tandoor-grilled chicken tikka pieces wrapped inside a soft wheat flatbread — typically a layered paratha or plain roti — along with sliced onions, green mint-coriander chutney, and a squeeze of lemon juice. The term 'malai' (Hindi: मलाई) refers to the rich cream and fresh cheese marinade in which the chicken is soaked before grilling, producing a distinctively mild, buttery, and slightly smoky flavour profile that contrasts with the more intensely spiced red tandoori tikka. The dish belongs to the kathi roll tradition originating in Kolkata but widely adopted across North Indian cuisine and now served throughout Singapore at North Indian restaurants. Nutritionally, one roll (~220 g) provides approximately 524 kcal, 32 g protein, 24 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate, making it a calorie-dense, protein-rich meal. The cream marinade contributes meaningful saturated fat (~9 g per roll), and the wheat paratha base gives a medium glycemic index of approximately 62. Sodium content is moderate to high (~924 mg per roll), relevant for hypertension management. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and is a good source of B vitamins, phosphorus, and iron from the chicken component.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for malai tikka roll as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries (tandoori chicken, wheat paratha, cream/malai) with USDA SR Legacy cross-check. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney wheat flatbread data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry covers malai tikka roll as a finished dish. Values should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for these components. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for these components. 4. Serving size of 220 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion weight for a single kathi roll; actual restaurant portions in Singapore may vary between 180–260 g. 5. Halal status is conditional on the restaurant using halal-certified chicken and dairy; both listed restaurants (Bombay Curry And Kebabs, Aryana) are North Indian halal-oriented establishments in Singapore. 6. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from wheat flatbread data in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for malai tikka roll was found in peer-reviewed literature. 7. Chinese names (马来提卡卷 / 馬來提卡卷) are transliterations used for retrieval purposes; the dish is not traditionally named in Chinese.
